From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Christian liturgical period

For the album by Bob Bennett, see [*Christmastide* (album)](/wiki/Christmastide_(album) "Christmastide (album)"). For the EP by Tori Amos, see [*Christmastide* (EP)](/wiki/Christmastide_(EP) "Christmastide (EP)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Christmas_Crib.jpg/250px-Christmas_Crib.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christmas_Crib.jpg)

A [nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene") and a [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree"), two popular decorations displayed by Christians during Christmastide

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Adoration_of_the_sheperds_-_Matthias_Stomer.jpg/250px-Adoration_of_the_sheperds_-_Matthias_Stomer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adoration_of_the_sheperds_-_Matthias_Stomer.jpg)

*Adoration of the Shepherds* by Dutch painter [Matthias Stomer](/wiki/Matthias_Stomer "Matthias Stomer"), 1632

**Christmastide**, also known as the **Christmas season**, **Christmastime**, **Christide**, is a season of the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in most [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") churches.

For the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church"), [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church "Anglican Church"), [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodist_Church "Methodist Church") and some Orthodox Churches, Christmastide begins on 24 December at sunset or [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers"), which is [liturgicus](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") the beginning of [Christmas Day](/wiki/Christmas_Day "Christmas Day").[[1]](#cite_note-Satter2005-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Hickman1984-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Hickman-4) Most of 24 December is thus not part of Christmastide, but of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"), the season in the Church Year that precedes Christmastide. In many liturgical calendars Christmastide is followed by the closely related season of [Epiphanytide](/wiki/Epiphanytide "Epiphanytide") that commences at sunset on 5 January—a date known as [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)").[[5]](#cite_note-ManganWyse2001-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Pennick2015-6)

In many [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"), Christmastide is identical to [Twelvetide](/wiki/Twelvetide "Twelvetide") (Twelve Days of Christmastide). There are several celebrations within Christmastide, including [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") Day (25 December), [Saint Stephen's Day](/wiki/Saint_Stephen%27s_Day "Saint Stephen's Day") (26 December), [Childermas](/wiki/Innocents_day "Innocents day") (28 December), [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve") (31 December), the [Feast of the Circumcision of Christ](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Circumcision_of_Christ "Feast of the Circumcision of Christ") or the [Solemnity of Mary, Mother of God](/wiki/Solemnity_of_Mary,_Mother_of_God "Solemnity of Mary, Mother of God") ([1 January](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Day "New Year's Day")), and the Feast of the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family") (date varies). The Twelve Days of Christmas terminate with [Epiphany Eve](/wiki/Epiphany_Eve "Epiphany Eve") or [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)") (the evening of 5 January).[[7]](#cite_note-Wickham2013-7)

Customs of the Christmas season include [carol singing](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol"), gift giving, attending [Nativity plays](/wiki/Nativity_play "Nativity play"), [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service"),[[8]](#cite_note-Bharati1976-8) and eating special food, such as [Christmas cake](/wiki/Christmas_cake "Christmas cake").[[9]](#cite_note-Nair2013-9) Traditional examples of Christmas greetings include the [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") phrase "Merry Christmas and a Happy New Year!" and the [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") greeting "Christ is born!", to which others respond, "Glorify Him!"[[10]](#cite_note-Dice2009-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Ruprecht2010-11)

## Dates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Dates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Adoration_assisi.jpg/250px-Adoration_assisi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adoration_assisi.jpg)

*[The Adoration of the Magi](/wiki/Adoration_of_the_Magi_in_art "Adoration of the Magi in art")*, [Fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") at the Lower Church of the [Basilica of San Francesco d'Assisi](/wiki/Basilica_of_San_Francesco_d%27Assisi "Basilica of San Francesco d'Assisi") in [Assisi](/wiki/Assisi "Assisi"), Italy

Christmastide, commonly called the [Twelve Days of Christmas](/wiki/Twelve_Days_of_Christmas "Twelve Days of Christmas"), lasts 12 days, from 25 December to 5 January, the latter date being named as [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)").[[12]](#cite_note-12) These traditional dates are adhered to by the [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church") and the [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion").[[1]](#cite_note-Satter2005-1)

However, the ending is defined differently by other Christian denominations.[[13]](#cite_note-Truscott-13) In 1969, the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") expanded Christmastide by a variable number of days: "Christmas Time runs from... up to and including the Sunday after Epiphany or after 6 January."[[14]](#cite_note-Norms33-14) Before 1955, the 12 Christmastide days in the Roman Rite (25 December to 5 January) were followed by the 8 days of the [Octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)") of Epiphany, 6–13 January, and its 1960 [Code of Rubrics](/wiki/Code_of_Rubrics "Code of Rubrics") defined "Christmastide" as running "from I vespers of Christmas to [none](/wiki/None_(liturgy) "None (liturgy)") of 5th January inclusive".[[15]](#cite_note-15) *The Saint Andrew Daily Missal* (1945) says Christmastide begins with "the vigil of the feast [Christmas Day] and ends in the temporal cycle on the octave day of the Epiphany...[and] in the sanctoral cycle on the Purification of our Lady (Feb. 2)."[[16]](#cite_note-Lefebvre1999-16) Within the Christmas Cycle is "the time before, during and after the feast itself, thus having for its aim to *prepare* the soul for them, then allow it to *celebrate* them with solemnity and finally to *prolong* them several weeks"; this references [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"), Christmas, and the [Time after Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphanytide "Epiphanytide") (Epiphanytide).[[16]](#cite_note-Lefebvre1999-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

| [Liturgical seasons](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") |
| --- |
| * Pre-Christmas   + [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") (*Western*)   + [Nativity Fast](/wiki/Nativity_Fast "Nativity Fast") (*Byzantine*)   + [Annunciation](/wiki/Season_of_Annunciation "Season of Annunciation") (*Syriac*) * Christmas * [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_season "Epiphany season")   + [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") (*Western*) * [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent") * [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (*Western*) / [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") (*Eastern*) * [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum") (*Western*) * [Easter](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") * [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost_season "Pentecost season")   + [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") (*Western*) * [Summer](/wiki/Season_of_Summer "Season of Summer") (*East Syriac*) * [Apostles' Fast](/wiki/Apostles%27_Fast "Apostles' Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Dormition Fast](/wiki/Dormition_Fast "Dormition Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Elijah–Cross–Moses](/w/index.php?title=Elijah%E2%80%93Cross%E2%80%93Moses&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elijah–Cross–Moses (page does not exist)") (*East Syriac*) * [Dedication of the Church](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_the_Dedication_of_the_Church&action=edit&redlink=1 "Season of the Dedication of the Church (page does not exist)") (*Syriac*) |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Liturgical_year "Template:Liturgical year") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Liturgical_year "Template talk:Liturgical year") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical_year "Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical year") |

In 567, the [Council of Tours](/wiki/Council_of_Tours_567 "Council of Tours 567") "proclaimed the twelve days from Christmas to Epiphany as a sacred and festive season, and established the duty of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") fasting in preparation for the feast."[[17]](#cite_note-Weiser-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Forbes2008-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Martindale-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Bunson-22) Christopher Hill, as well as William J. Federer, states that this was done in order to solve the "administrative problem for the Roman Empire as it tried to coordinate the solar Julian calendar with the lunar calendars of its provinces in the east."[[23]](#cite_note-Hill2003-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Ronald Hutton](/wiki/Ronald_Hutton "Ronald Hutton") adds that, while the Council of Tours declared the 12 days one festal cycle, it confirmed that three of those days were fasting days, dividing the rejoicing days into two blocs.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Dowden-28)

In [medieval era](/wiki/History_of_Christianity_of_the_Middle_Ages "History of Christianity of the Middle Ages") Christendom, Christmastide "lasted from the Nativity to the [Purification](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple")."[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-PhanBrancatelli2005-30) To this day, the "Christian cultures in Western Europe and Latin America extend the season to forty days, ending on the Feast of the Presentation of Jesus in the Temple and the Purification of Mary on 2 February, a feast also known as [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas") because of the blessing of candles on this day, inspired by the [Song of Simeon](/wiki/Song_of_Simeon "Song of Simeon"), which proclaims Jesus as 'a light for revelation to the nations'."[[31]](#cite_note-Senn2012-31) Many Churches refer to the period after the traditional Twelve Days of Christmas and up to Candlemas, as [Epiphanytide](/wiki/Epiphanytide "Epiphanytide"), also called the Epiphany season.[[32]](#cite_note-CRI-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Atwell2013-33) The [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") referred to the season as **Christide** as they did not affirm the sacrificial aspect of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Traditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Complete-church-midnight-mass_%283135957575%29.jpg/250px-Complete-church-midnight-mass_%283135957575%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Complete-church-midnight-mass_(3135957575).jpg)

Many Christians attend [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") to celebrate the [birth of Jesus Christ](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus").[[35]](#cite_note-Jespersen2011-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Elim2.jpg/250px-Elim2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elim2.jpg)

The [Moravian star](/wiki/Moravian_star "Moravian star") is a common decoration seen in many Christian households and churches, especially those of [Moravians](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), during Christmastide and Epiphanytide

During the Christmas season, various festivities are traditionally enjoyed and buildings are adorned with [Christmas decorations](/wiki/Christmas_decorations "Christmas decorations"), which are often set up during [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent").[[36]](#cite_note-Michelin2012-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) These Christmas decorations include the [Nativity Scene](/wiki/Nativity_Scene "Nativity Scene"), [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree"), [Moravian star](/wiki/Moravian_star "Moravian star"), [Illuminations](/wiki/Illumination_(decoration) "Illumination (decoration)") and various [Christmas ornaments](/wiki/Christmas_ornament "Christmas ornament"). In the Western Christian world, the two traditional days on which Christmas decorations are removed are [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)") and [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas").[[38]](#cite_note-RTE2026-38) Any not removed on the first occasion should be left undisturbed until the second.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Removing Christmas decorations before Twelfth Night (5 January) and leaving the decorations up beyond Candlemas is considered to be inauspicious.[[38]](#cite_note-RTE2026-38)[[40]](#cite_note-Raedisch2013-40) *The Saint Andrew Daily Missal* (1945), authored by [Dom Gaspar Lefebvre](/wiki/Gaspar_Lefebvre "Gaspar Lefebvre"), stipulates:[[16]](#cite_note-Lefebvre1999-16)

> Every Christian home should have its own little crib round which, on these days, morning and evening prayers should be said. At this season, consecrated to childlike joys, children will understand that they must join with the shepherds and the wise men together with Mary and Joseph in worshipping the Child Jesus, the Babe who lying on His bed of straw is God and beseech Him that through His grace they may become ever increasingly children of God together with Him. The greetings of "Happy Christmas" which remind us of the artless mirth of the shepherds on that holy night; the Christmas tree, often with a source of joy to the poor, representatives of Christ in the property of His manger bed; Christmas gifts recalling God's great gift of His Son to us on the first Christmas night; the Twelfth-Night cake; all these are Christian customs which ought to be preserved. —The Saint Andrew Daily Missal[[16]](#cite_note-Lefebvre1999-16)

On [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") or [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") Day (the first day of Christmastide), it is customary for most households in [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") to attend a [service of worship](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") or [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)").[[41]](#cite_note-Aloian2008-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Altar1885-42) During the season of Christmastide, in many Christian households, a gift is given for each of the [Twelve Days of Christmastide](/wiki/Twelve_Days_of_Christmas "Twelve Days of Christmas"), while in others, gifts are only given on [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve"), [Christmas Day](/wiki/Christmas_Day "Christmas Day") or [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)"), the first and last days of the festive season, respectively.[[43]](#cite_note-Kubesh-43) The practice of giving gifts during Christmastide, according to Christian tradition, is symbolic of the [presentation of the gifts](/wiki/Adoration_of_the_Magi "Adoration of the Magi") by the [Three Wise Men](/wiki/Biblical_Magi "Biblical Magi") to the [infant Jesus](/wiki/Christ_Child "Christ Child").[[44]](#cite_note-BashBash2012-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Carnegie_Presepio.JPG/250px-Carnegie_Presepio.JPG)](/wiki/File:Carnegie_Presepio.JPG)

[Neapolitan *presepio*](/wiki/Neapolitan_nativity_scene "Neapolitan nativity scene") at the [Carnegie Museum of Art](/wiki/Carnegie_Museum_of_Art "Carnegie Museum of Art") in Pittsburgh

The tradition of the [Nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene") comes from Italy. One of the earliest representation in art of the nativity was found in the [early Christian](/wiki/Early_Christian "Early Christian") [Roman catacomb](/wiki/Roman_catacomb "Roman catacomb") of [Saint Valentine](/wiki/Catacombs_of_San_Valentino "Catacombs of San Valentino").[[45]](#cite_note-Osborne2020-45) It dates to about AD 380.[[46]](#cite_note-Tuleja1999-46) Another, of similar date, is beneath the pulpit in [Sant'Ambrogio, Milan](/wiki/Basilica_of_Sant%27Ambrogio "Basilica of Sant'Ambrogio"). For the Christian celebration of Christmas, the viewing of the [Nativity play](/wiki/Nativity_play "Nativity play") is one of the oldest Christmastime traditions, with the first reenactment of the [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") taking place in A.D. 1223 in the Italian town of [Greccio](/wiki/Greccio "Greccio").[[47]](#cite_note-Collins2010-47) In that year, [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") assembled a Nativity scene outside of his church in Italy and children sang Christmas carols celebrating the birth of Jesus.[[47]](#cite_note-Collins2010-47) Each year, this grew larger, and people travelled from afar to see Francis' depiction of the Nativity of Jesus that came to feature drama and music.[[47]](#cite_note-Collins2010-47) Nativity plays eventually spread throughout all of Europe, where they remain popular. Christmas Eve and Christmas Day church services often came to feature Nativity plays, as did schools and theatres.[[47]](#cite_note-Collins2010-47) In France, Germany, Mexico, and Spain, Nativity plays are often reenacted outdoors in the streets.[[47]](#cite_note-Collins2010-47)

In several parts of the world, it is common to have a large family feast on Christmas Day, preceded by saying [grace](/wiki/Grace_(prayer) "Grace (prayer)"). Desserts such as [Christmas cake](/wiki/Christmas_cake "Christmas cake") are unique to Christmastide; in India and Pakistan, a version known as [Allahabadi cake](/wiki/Allahabadi_cake "Allahabadi cake") is popular.[[9]](#cite_note-Nair2013-9) *[Panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone")*, an [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine") type of [sweet bread](/wiki/List_of_sweet_breads "List of sweet breads") and [fruitcake](/wiki/Fruitcake "Fruitcake"), originally from [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy, usually prepared and enjoyed for Christmas and [New Year](/wiki/New_Year "New Year") in [Western](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), and [Southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe"), as well as in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea"),[[48]](#cite_note-48) Australia and North America.[[49]](#cite_note-49) During the Christmas season, it is also very common for [Christmas carols](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol") to be sung at Christian churches, as well as in front of houses—in the latter scenario, groups of Christians go from one house to another to sing Christmas carols.[[50]](#cite_note-GeddesGriffiths2002-50) Popular Christmas carols include "[Silent Night](/wiki/Silent_Night "Silent Night")", "[Come, Thou Long Expected Jesus](/wiki/Come,_Thou_Long_Expected_Jesus "Come, Thou Long Expected Jesus")", "[We Three Kings](/wiki/We_Three_Kings "We Three Kings")", "[Down in Yon Forest](/wiki/Down_in_Yon_Forest "Down in Yon Forest")", "[Away in a Manger](/wiki/Away_in_a_Manger "Away in a Manger")", "[I Wonder as I Wander](/wiki/I_Wonder_as_I_Wander "I Wonder as I Wander")", "[God Rest Ye Merry, Gentlemen](/wiki/God_Rest_Ye_Merry,_Gentlemen "God Rest Ye Merry, Gentlemen")", "[There's a Song in the Air](/wiki/There%27s_a_Song_in_the_Air "There's a Song in the Air")", and "[Let all mortal flesh keep silence](/wiki/Let_all_mortal_flesh_keep_silence "Let all mortal flesh keep silence")".[[51]](#cite_note-Parker2005-51) In the Christmas season, it is very common for television stations to air feature films relating to Christmas and Christianity in general, such as *[The Greatest Story Ever Told](/wiki/The_Greatest_Story_Ever_Told "The Greatest Story Ever Told")* and *[Scrooge](/wiki/Scrooge_(1951_film) "Scrooge (1951 film)")*.[[52]](#cite_note-Newman1996-52)

On [Saint Stephen's Day](/wiki/Saint_Stephen%27s_Day "Saint Stephen's Day"), the second day of Christmastide,[[53]](#cite_note-Lopez1977-53) people traditionally have their [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse") blessed,[[54]](#cite_note-Walsh2001-54) and on the [Feast of Saint John the Evangelist](/wiki/John_the_Evangelist "John the Evangelist"), the third day of Christmastide,[[55]](#cite_note-Dame2006-55) wine is blessed and consumed.[[54]](#cite_note-Walsh2001-54) The fourth day of Christmastide, [Childermas](/wiki/Childermas "Childermas") (Children's Mass), is observed through the blessing of children at church, as well as the remembrance of the Holy Innocents as the first [Christian martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) On [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve") (the seventh day of Christmastide), it is common for many Christians to attend a [watchnight service](/wiki/Watchnight_service "Watchnight service") to thank God for being blessed in the previous year and resolving to serve Him in the coming year.[[58]](#cite_note-Lawbaugh2016-58) Throughout the twelve days of Christmastide, many people view [Nativity plays](/wiki/Nativity_play "Nativity play"),[[59]](#cite_note-Martin1959-59) among other forms of "musical and theatrical presentations".[[54]](#cite_note-Walsh2001-54)

In the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), Christmastide is referred to as "Svyatki", meaning "Holy Days". It is celebrated from the Nativity of Christ (7 January [N.S.](/wiki/New_Style "New Style")) to the Theophany or Baptism of Christ (19 January N.S.). Activities during this period include attending church services, singing Christmas carols and spiritual hymns, visiting relatives and friends, and performing works of mercy, such as visiting the sick, the elderly people, orphans, and giving generous alms.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/RIAN_archive_1011996_Christmas_service_at_the_Cathedral_of_Christ_the_Saviour.jpg/250px-RIAN_archive_1011996_Christmas_service_at_the_Cathedral_of_Christ_the_Saviour.jpg)](/wiki/File:RIAN_archive_1011996_Christmas_service_at_the_Cathedral_of_Christ_the_Saviour.jpg)

Christmas service at the [Cathedral of Christ the Saviour](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Christ_the_Saviour "Cathedral of Christ the Saviour"), [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")

In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), as well as in the [Greek Catholic Churches](/wiki/Greek_Catholic_Churches "Greek Catholic Churches") and [Byzantine-Rite Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite_Lutheranism "Byzantine Rite Lutheranism"), Christmas is the fourth most important feast (after [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") and [Theophany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)")). The day after, the Church celebrates the [Synaxis](/wiki/Synaxis "Synaxis") of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"). This means that [Saint Stephen's Day](/wiki/Saint_Stephen%27s_Day "Saint Stephen's Day") and the [Feast of the Holy Innocents](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Holy_Innocents "Feast of the Holy Innocents") fall one day later than in the West. The coming of the [Wise Men](/wiki/Magi "Magi") is celebrated on the feast itself.

[Panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone")

Christmas is one of the [major holidays of Italy](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Italy "Public holidays in Italy"). [Christmas festivities in Italy](/wiki/Christmas_in_Italy "Christmas in Italy") ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Natale*, Italian: [[naˈtaːle]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) begin on 8 December, with the [Feast of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Feast of the Immaculate Conception"), the day on which traditionally the [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") is mounted and ends on 6 January, of the following year with the [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") (Italian: *Epifania*, Italian: [[epifaˈniːa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")),[[68]](#cite_note-:2-68) and in some areas female puppets are burned on a pyre (called *[falò](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire")*), to symbolize, along with the end of the Christmas period, the death of the old year and the beginning of a new one.

## Suppression by antireligious governments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Suppression by antireligious governments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Bezbozhnik_u_stanka_-_Run_along%2C_Lord%2C_1931%2C_n._22.jpg/250px-Bezbozhnik_u_stanka_-_Run_along%2C_Lord%2C_1931%2C_n._22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bezbozhnik_u_stanka_-_Run_along,_Lord,_1931,_n._22.jpg)

A 1931 edition of the Soviet magazine *[Bezbozhnik](/wiki/Bezbozhnik_(magazine) "Bezbozhnik (magazine)")*, published by the [League of Militant Atheists](/wiki/League_of_Militant_Atheists "League of Militant Atheists"), depicting an Orthodox Christian priest being forbidden to take home a tree for the celebration of Christmastide, which was banned under the Marxist-Leninist doctrine of [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism").[[69]](#cite_note-Harper1999-69)

### Revolutionary France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Revolutionary France")]

Further information: [Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution](/wiki/Dechristianization_of_France_during_the_French_Revolution "Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution")

With the atheistic [Cult of Reason](/wiki/Cult_of_Reason "Cult of Reason") in power during the era of [Revolutionary France](/wiki/Revolutionary_France "Revolutionary France"), Christian Christmas [religious services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") were banned and the [three kings cake](/wiki/Three_kings_cake "Three kings cake") of the Christmas-Epiphany season was forcibly renamed the "equality cake" under [anticlerical government policies](/wiki/Dechristianization_of_France_during_the_French_Revolution "Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution").[[70]](#cite_note-Inc1996-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Soviet Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Soviet Union")]

Further information: [Persecution of Christians in the Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_Eastern_Bloc "Persecution of Christians in the Eastern Bloc") and [Soviet anti-religious legislation](/wiki/Soviet_anti-religious_legislation "Soviet anti-religious legislation")

Under the [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), after its foundation in 1917, Christmas celebrations—along with other Christian holidays—were prohibited. [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas") was replaced by [Ded Moroz](/wiki/Ded_Moroz "Ded Moroz") or [Grandfather Frost](/wiki/Father_Frost "Father Frost"), the Russian Spirit of Winter who brought gifts on New Year's, accompanied by the snowmaiden *Snyegurochka* who helps distribute gifts.[[72]](#cite_note-DeLaine-72)

It was not until the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991 that the prohibition ended and Christmas was celebrated for the first time in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") after seven decades.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Russia had adopted the custom of celebrating New Year's Day instead. However, the Orthodox Church Christmas is on 7 January. This is, also, an [official national holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Russia "Public holidays in Russia").[[72]](#cite_note-DeLaine-72)

### Nazi Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Nazi Germany")]

Further information: [Kirchenkampf](/wiki/Kirchenkampf "Kirchenkampf")

European History Professor Joseph Perry wrote that in [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), "because Nazi ideologues saw organized religion as an enemy of the totalitarian state, propagandists sought to deemphasize—or eliminate altogether—the Christian aspects of the holiday" and that "Propagandists tirelessly promoted numerous Nazified Christmas songs, which replaced Christian themes with the regime's racial ideologies."[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### People's Republic of China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmastide&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: People's Republic of China")]

Further information: [Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Antireligious_campaigns_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party")

The government of the People's Republic of China officially espouses [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism"),[[75]](#cite_note-75) and has conducted [antireligious campaigns](/wiki/Antireligious_campaigns_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party") to this end.[[76]](#cite_note-BuangChew2014-76) In December 2018, officials raided Christian churches just prior to Christmastide and coerced them to close; Christmas trees and Santa Clauses were also forcibly removed.[[77]](#cite_note-BBC2018-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)
